question: A-6. Determine all polynomials of the form \( \sum_{0}^{n} a_{i} x^{n-i} \) with \( a_{i}= \pm 1(0 \leqq i \leqq n, 1 \leqq n<\infty) \) such that each has only real zeros.
solution:
A-6 (0) The desired polynomials with \( a_{0}=-1 \) are the negative of those with \( a_{0}=1 \), so consider \( a_{0}=1 \). The sum of the squares of the zeros of \( x^{n}+a_{1} x^{n-1}+\cdots \) \( +a_{n} \) is \( a_{1}^{2}-2 a_{2} \). The product of the squares of these zeros is \( a_{n}^{2} \). If all the zeros are real, we can apply the arithmetic-geometric mean inequality to obtain
\[
\frac{a_{1}^{2}-2 a_{2}}{n} \geqq\left(a_{n}^{2}\right)^{1 / n}
\]
with equality only if the zeros are numerically equal. In our case this inequality becomes \( (1 \pm 2) / n \geqq 1 \) or \( n \leqq 3 \). Note that \( n>1 \) implies \( a_{2}=-1 \) and \( n=3 \) implies all zeros are \( \pm 1 \). Thus the list of polynomials is:
\[
\begin{array}{c} 
\pm(x-1), \quad \pm(x+1), \quad \pm\left(x^{2}+x-1\right), \quad \pm\left(x^{2}-x-1\right), \\
\pm\left(x^{3}+x^{2}-x-1\right), \quad \pm\left(x^{3}-x^{2}-x+1\right) .
\end{array}
\]

descriptive_long.question: A-6. Determine all polynomials of the form \( \sum_{0}^{degcount} coeffindex \, variable^{degcount-indexer} \) with \( coeffindex= \pm 1(0 \leqq indexer \leqq degcount, 1 \leqq degcount<\infty) \) such that each has only real zeros.
descriptive_long.solution:
A-6 (0) The desired polynomials with \( coeffzero=-1 \) are the negative of those with \( coeffzero=1 \), so consider \( coeffzero=1 \). The sum of the squares of the zeros of \( variable^{degcount}+coeffone \, variable^{degcount-1}+\cdots +coefflast \) is \( coeffone^{2}-2 coefftwo \). The product of the squares of these zeros is \( coefflast^{2} \). If all the zeros are real, we can apply the arithmetic-geometric mean inequality to obtain
\[
\frac{coeffone^{2}-2 coefftwo}{degcount} \geqq\left(coefflast^{2}\right)^{1 / degcount}
\]
with equality only if the zeros are numerically equal. In our case this inequality becomes \( (1 \pm 2) / degcount \geqq 1 \) or \( degcount \leqq 3 \). Note that \( degcount>1 \) implies \( coefftwo=-1 \) and \( degcount=3 \) implies all zeros are \( \pm 1 \). Thus the list of polynomials is:
\[
\begin{array}{c} 
\pm(variable-1), \quad \pm(variable+1), \quad \pm\left(variable^{2}+variable-1\right), \quad \pm\left(variable^{2}-variable-1\right), \\
\pm\left(variable^{3}+variable^{2}-variable-1\right), \quad \pm\left(variable^{3}-variable^{2}-variable+1\right) .
\end{array}
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question: A-6. Determine all polynomials of the form \( \sum_{0}^{bluegrass} buttercup marinade^{bluegrass-cantaloup} \) with \( buttercup= \pm 1(0 \leqq cantaloup \leqq bluegrass, 1 \leqq bluegrass<\infty) \) such that each has only real zeros.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-6 (0) The desired polynomials with \( dandelion=-1 \) are the negative of those with \( dandelion=1 \), so consider \( dandelion=1 \). The sum of the squares of the zeros of \( marinade^{bluegrass}+honeysuck marinade^{bluegrass-1}+\cdots \) \( +snapdragon \) is \( honeysuck^{2}-2 lilywhite \). The product of the squares of these zeros is \( snapdragon^{2} \). If all the zeros are real, we can apply the arithmetic-geometric mean inequality to obtain
\[\frac{honeysuck^{2}-2 lilywhite}{bluegrass} \geqq\left(snapdragon^{2}\right)^{1 / bluegrass}\]
with equality only if the zeros are numerically equal. In our case this inequality becomes \( (1 \pm 2) / bluegrass \geqq 1 \) or \( bluegrass \leqq 3 \). Note that \( bluegrass>1 \) implies \( lilywhite=-1 \) and \( bluegrass=3 \) implies all zeros are \( \pm 1 \). Thus the list of polynomials is:
\[\begin{array}{c} 
\pm(marinade-1), \quad \pm(marinade+1), \quad \pm\left(marinade^{2}+marinade-1\right), \quad \pm\left(marinade^{2}-marinade-1\right), \\
\pm\left(marinade^{3}+marinade^{2}-marinade-1\right), \quad \pm\left(marinade^{3}-marinade^{2}-marinade+1\right) .
\end{array}\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question: A-6. Determine all polynomials of the form \( \sum_{0}^{boundless} variableco\, unchanging^{boundless-complete} \) with \( variableco = \pm 1(0 \leqq complete \leqq boundless, 1 \leqq boundless < \infty) \) such that each has only real zeros.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-6 (0) The desired polynomials with \( movingcore=-1 \) are the negative of those with \( movingcore=1 \), so consider \( movingcore=1 \). The sum of the squares of the zeros of \( unchanging^{boundless}+shiftstart\, unchanging^{boundless-1}+\cdots \) \( +shiftend \) is \( shiftstart^{2}-2 shiftsecond \). The product of the squares of these zeros is \( shiftend^{2} \). If all the zeros are real, we can apply the arithmetic-geometric mean inequality to obtain
\[
\frac{shiftstart^{2}-2 shiftsecond}{boundless} \geqq \left(shiftend^{2}\right)^{1 / boundless}
\]
with equality only if the zeros are numerically equal. In our case this inequality becomes \( (1 \pm 2) / boundless \geqq 1 \) or \( boundless \leqq 3 \). Note that \( boundless>1 \) implies \( shiftsecond=-1 \) and \( boundless=3 \) implies all zeros are \( \pm 1 \). Thus the list of polynomials is:
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\pm(unchanging-1), \quad \pm(unchanging+1), \quad \pm\left(unchanging^{2}+unchanging-1\right), \quad \pm\left(unchanging^{2}-unchanging-1\right), \\
\pm\left(unchanging^{3}+unchanging^{2}-unchanging-1\right), \quad \pm\left(unchanging^{3}-unchanging^{2}-unchanging+1\right) .
\end{array}
\]

garbled_string.question: A-6. Determine all polynomials of the form \( \sum_{0}^{hjgrksla} vsnejrpa qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla-rmdfplkc} \) with \( vsnejrpa= \pm 1(0 \leqq rmdfplkc \leqq hjgrksla, 1 \leqq hjgrksla<\infty) \) such that each has only real zeros.
garbled_string.solution:
A-6 (0) The desired polynomials with \( pdxkqlru=-1 \) are the negative of those with \( pdxkqlru=1 \), so consider \( pdxkqlru=1 \). The sum of the squares of the zeros of \( qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla}+fqnzmoch qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla-1}+\cdots +kavshude \) is \( fqnzmoch^{2}-2 tgrylxse \). The product of the squares of these zeros is \( kavshude^{2} \). If all the zeros are real, we can apply the arithmetic-geometric mean inequality to obtain
\[
\frac{fqnzmoch^{2}-2 tgrylxse}{hjgrksla} \geqq\left(kavshude^{2}\right)^{1 / hjgrksla}
\]
with equality only if the zeros are numerically equal. In our case this inequality becomes \( (1 \pm 2) / hjgrksla \geqq 1 \) or \( hjgrksla \leqq 3 \). Note that \( hjgrksla>1 \) implies \( tgrylxse=-1 \) and \( hjgrksla=3 \) implies all zeros are \( \pm 1 \). Thus the list of polynomials is:
\[
\begin{array}{c} 
\pm(qzxwvtnp-1), \quad \pm(qzxwvtnp+1), \quad \pm\left(qzxwvtnp^{2}+qzxwvtnp-1\right), \quad \pm\left(qzxwvtnp^{2}-qzxwvtnp-1\right), \\
\pm\left(qzxwvtnp^{3}+qzxwvtnp^{2}-qzxwvtnp-1\right), \quad \pm\left(qzxwvtnp^{3}-qzxwvtnp^{2}-qzxwvtnp+1\right) .
\end{array}
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let
P(x)= -x^{n}+a_{1}x^{n-1}+a_{2}x^{n-2}+\cdots +a_{n-1}x-1 \qquad(n\ge 1),
where each coefficient satisfies a_{k}\in\{\pm 1\}.
(Thus both the leading coefficient and the constant term of P are equal to -1.)
Determine all such polynomials whose zeros are all real numbers.
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  Replace P by a monic polynomial.
Set
   Q(x)= -P(x)=x^{n}+b_{1}x^{n-1}+b_{2}x^{n-2}+\cdots +b_{n-1}x+1,
where b_{k}=-a_{k}\;(1\le k\le n-1).  Each b_{k} equals \pm1 and the constant term of Q is +1.  Because the roots of P and Q coincide, it suffices to find all Q of the above shape whose n zeros r_{1},\dots ,r_{n} are real.

Step 2.  A general inequality for n\ge 3.
Assume first that n\ge 3 so that both a_{1} and a_{2} (hence b_{1},b_{2}) are present.
By Vieta,
  \sum_{k=1}^{n} r_{k}= -b_{1}= a_{1},
  \sum_{1\le i<j\le n} r_{i}r_{j}= b_{2}= -a_{2},
  \prod_{k=1}^{n} r_{k}= (-1)^{n} \,(\text{coefficient of }x^{0})= (-1)^{n}.
Hence
  \sum_{k=1}^{n} r_{k}^{2}= (\sum r_{k})^{2}-2 \sum_{i<j} r_{i}r_{j}
                        = a_{1}^{2}-2(-a_{2})
                        = 1+2a_{2},
while
  \prod_{k=1}^{n} r_{k}^{2}=1.
Because all r_{k}^{2}\ge 0, the arithmetic-geometric-mean (AM-GM) inequality gives
   (\sum r_{k}^{2})/n \ge (\prod r_{k}^{2})^{1/n}=1,\qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad 1+2a_{2}\ge n.
Since a_{2}=\pm1, 1+2a_{2} equals 3 or -1.  The inequality can hold only for 1+2a_{2}=3 and n\le3.  As we are in the case n\ge3, we must have
               n=3 \quad\text{and}\quad a_{2}=+1 (equivalently, b_{2}=-1).

Step 3.  Examine n=3.
With n=3 we have
   Q(x)=x^{3}+b_{1}x^{2}+b_{2}x+1=x^{3}+b_{1}x^{2}-x+1,\quad b_{1}=\pm1.
(a)  b_{1}=1:
       Q(x)=x^{3}+x^{2}-x+1.
     A cubic always has at least one real zero; here the discriminant is negative, so exactly one zero is real and two are complex, hence not all three zeros are real.
(b)  b_{1}=-1:
       Q(x)=x^{3}-x^{2}-x+1=(x-1)^{2}(x+1).
     All three zeros (1,1,-1) are real.  Thus this choice works.
Therefore, for n=3 the unique acceptable polynomial is
    Q(x)=x^{3}-x^{2}-x+1,\quad\text{hence}\quad P(x)=-x^{3}+x^{2}+x-1.

Step 4.  Examine n=2 separately.
If n=2 then Q(x)=x^{2}+b_{1}x+1 with b_{1}=\pm1.  Its discriminant is
   \Delta=b_{1}^{2}-4=1-4=-3<0,
so Q has two non-real conjugate zeros.  Thus no quadratic of the required form has all real zeros.

Step 5.  Examine n=1.
For n=1 we have Q(x)=x+1, whose single zero -1 is real.  Consequently
   P(x)=-x-1.

Step 6.  Collect the answers.
Hence the polynomials satisfying the stated conditions are
   P_{1}(x)= -x-1  \quad(n=1),
   P_{2}(x)= -x^{3}+x^{2}+x-1 \quad(n=3).
Both indeed have only real zeros (respectively -1 and 1,1,-1), completing the classification.

core_steps: Use Vieta:  Σ(roots²) = a₁² − 2a₂  and  Π(roots²) = a_n²
Apply AGM to the squares of real roots:  (a₁²−2a₂)/n ≥ (a_n²)^{1/n}
Substitute a_i = ±1 to get (1±2)/n ≥ 1 ⇒ n ≤ 3 and force a₂ = –1, equality only when all roots = ±1
List the degree-1, 2, 3 sign patterns that satisfy the above and re-adjoin the overall ± sign

slot1.description: Overall sign of the polynomial (multiply every coefficient by −1)

slot2.description: Sign of the constant coefficient a_n; its square remains 1 so proof unchanged
